background_system.philosophy: Backgrounds are starting contexts, not classes. They describe where you woke up, what you know how to do, and who might recognize your face. They are not ceilings. A levy soldier can become a merchant. A baker can learn the sword. Background sets the opening hand — the player decides what to do with it.
background_system.skill_model: Mixed. A signature cluster at meaningful starting level (can do the thing, not just attempt it). A wider spread of traces (aware of the thing, has touched it, improves faster than a complete novice).
background_system.social_standing_model: Partial. Background affects specific factions and regions only. Universal reputation is earned entirely in-play.

skill_levels_reference.0: No knowledge. Attempts are raw improvisation.
skill_levels_reference.1: Trace. Aware of the basics. Improves faster than zero.
skill_levels_reference.2: Apprentice. Can perform simple tasks reliably. Makes beginner mistakes on complex ones.
skill_levels_reference.3: Journeyman. Competent. Earns wages. Rarely embarrasses themselves.
skill_levels_reference.4: Skilled. Noticeably better than average. Minor reputation in the trade.
skill_levels_reference.5: Expert. Sought out. Regional reputation. Years of focused work to reach.
skill_levels_reference.10: Master. Rare. Defining practitioner of the skill in a region.

backgrounds.name: Levy Soldier
backgrounds.hook: You were pulled from your village by a lord's draft and handed a spear. You survived whatever they sent you into. Now your service is up — or you walked away before it was.

signature_skills.note: You've drilled with it and used it in anger at least once
You know how to hold a line and not get your comrades killed
Long marches with kit do something to a body
A soldier who lets his weapon rust gets flogged

trace_skills.note: Camp cooking — barely qualifies
Field dressing, nothing more
Side-arm familiarity only

starting_inventory: Worn spear (functional, not pretty)
Padded gambeson (light armor, well-used)
Soldier's pack (bedroll, mess kit, small tools)
Two weeks back pay in iron chits (if honorably discharged) OR nothing (if deserted)

warm: Military garrisons (recognized as one of their own)
Reclaimed Path temples (soldiers are their flock in Cauldenmoor)
cold: Merchants (soldiers are trouble and theft risk)
Noble households (a levy is not a man-at-arms)
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Ironmark Borderlands, a former soldier is economically valuable from day one. In the Amber Coast, they are slightly suspicious without affiliation.

backgrounds.background_thread: Your unit. Whether you left honorably or not, they exist somewhere. Some of them know you. Some of them may have opinions about how you left. One of them owes you something, or you owe them.
backgrounds.the_weight: You know how to fight in a formation. Alone, in an alley, against three men, against a mounted rider — your training doesn't fully apply and you know it. The gap between what you were trained for and what the world actually presents is one of the first things you'll feel.

backgrounds.name: Disgraced Man-at-Arms
backgrounds.hook: You had rank once. You wore a lord's colors and were paid well to do it. You lost it — the reason is yours, but the record exists somewhere and precedes you in certain places.

signature_skills.note: Professionally trained. This is the real thing.
You wore it for years. You know its limits.
You dealt with lords and their households daily

trace_skills.note: You rode. You may not have a horse anymore.
Basic — orders, lists, simple correspondence

starting_inventory: Good sword (the one thing you kept)
Worn but quality clothing (not armor — you sold or pawned it)
A letter that you do not show people (your discharge, your warrant, or something worse)
Enough bronze marks for two weeks if careful

warm: Other former soldiers (professional recognition)
Mercenary companies (your skills are legible to them)
cold: The lord or house that disgraced you — actively hostile in their territories
Noble households generally (a disgraced man-at-arms is a warning sign)
social_standing.regional_notes: In Thornsveld Marches, your name may mean something specific — check whether Aldric IV's court is on your bad side. In the Ironmark Borderlands, nobody cares about your past if you can fight.

backgrounds.background_thread: The disgrace. It happened, it was recorded, and there is at least one person in the world who was directly affected by it. They haven't forgotten. The reason for your disgrace is either something you did, something you refused to do, or something that was done to you and blamed on you.
starting_zone_bias: Amber Coast (if the disgrace was far away)
backgrounds.the_weight: You are trained above your current station. Every lord's man who sees you work will wonder why you're not in service. Some will ask. The answer you give, and whether it's true, will follow you.

backgrounds.name: Camp Follower
backgrounds.hook: You grew up trailing armies — cooking, mending, carrying, trading, occasionally stealing. You've never held rank and you've seen more of war than most soldiers.

signature_skills.note: Feeding fifty men on bad supplies is a skill
You traded for everything in places where supply was scarce
Reading crowds, reading danger, reading who has what

trace_skills.note: You've dressed a lot of wounds
You understand supply chains from the bottom
You can read a room and adjust
Mostly mule and horse care for the column

starting_inventory: Good knife (tool, not weapon — though it works as either)
Cookpot and basic kit
Several useful small items acquired over time (rope, whetstone, two candles, a needle and thread)
Modest iron chits — enough for a few days

warm: Soldiers and mercenaries (they know what you are and that you're useful)
Market traders (you're one of them in spirit)
cold: Noble households (camp followers are the lowest social stratum in military culture)
Temples (depending on how you earned coin — some camp followers did sex work, which certain faiths record)
social_standing.regional_notes: The Ironmark Borderlands is natural territory. In the Amber Coast, your market skills translate well but your background reads as very low-class.

backgrounds.background_thread: The army you followed. It still exists somewhere, or its remnants do. There are people in it who know you, owe you favors, or have complicated feelings about you. One of them has something of yours, or you have something of theirs.
backgrounds.the_weight: You have no martial training at all. You have survived near violence your entire life through awareness and avoidance. The first time you can't avoid it, you'll find out what you're made of without a weapon skill to fall back on.

backgrounds.name: Dispossessed Farmhand
backgrounds.hook: The land you worked is gone — your lord died and the estate was seized, or the village burned, or the harvest failed three years running and people scattered. You walked away with a bundle.

signature_skills.note: Agricultural labor builds a specific kind of durability

trace_skills.note: Farmstead repair — rough work

starting_inventory: Working clothes (durable, worn)
A small bundle of personal items
A hand tool (hoe, small axe, or billhook — your choice)
Three days of food
Iron chits — barely enough for one night's lodging

warm: Other agricultural laborers (immediate solidarity)
Furrow faith temples (their people, their community)
cold: Urban merchants and guilds (you read as unskilled rural labor)
Noble households (invisible at best)
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Free Fens, agricultural knowledge is respected and there's always need for someone who can work land. In the Keth Plateau, your skills are almost entirely useless — nothing grows there.

backgrounds.background_thread: The place you came from. It exists — ruined, seized, or simply emptied — and there may be people from it scattered across the region. Someone from your village is somewhere. Your old lord's family is somewhere. The land itself is somewhere, and it's owned by someone now.
backgrounds.the_weight: You have no urban skills, no social connections, no literacy, no weapons training. What you have is the ability to work harder and longer than almost anyone, and the knowledge that land means survival. Finding land to work — or someday owning it — will be a constant pull.

backgrounds.name: Blacksmith's Apprentice
backgrounds.hook: You spent years at the forge — starting with sweeping and bellows-pumping, graduating to basic work. You know the smell of hot iron and what it means when the color isn't right.

signature_skills.note: Journeyman-level general smithing. Bladesmith sub-skill at 2.
Forge work builds specific and genuine strength
You know what good metal looks like and how to preserve it

trace_skills.note: Forge construction and tool handle work
Front-of-shop experience
You made them. You tested them. You're not trained.

starting_inventory: Basic smith's toolkit (hammer, tongs, files — personal tools, not a full shop setup)
Leather apron
One weapon of your own making (functional, simple — a knife or short blade)
Bronze marks — a few weeks of wages saved

warm: Craftsmen and guild members (professional recognition)
Soldiers and mercenaries (anyone who needs edge work done respects a smith)
cold: Nobody specific — smiths are universally useful
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Thornsveld Marches, the Durnwald Mine situation makes anyone with metal knowledge slightly more valuable than usual. Everywhere, a competent smith can find work within a day.

backgrounds.background_thread: Your master. The forge you trained at. Whether you left on good terms, finished your apprenticeship, or walked away early defines what you owe and who might track you down about it. Your master taught you something specific — a technique, a contact, a secret about a commission — that has value you may not yet understand.
backgrounds.the_weight: You are genuinely skilled at something the world always needs. The temptation to stop moving and set up a forge is real and immediate. The question of whether staying in one place is safety or a trap will follow you.

backgrounds.name: Tanner's Apprentice
backgrounds.hook: You know the smell of rot. You know which merchants buy leather, how to read the quality of a hide at a glance, and what happens to a person who handles certain chemicals without care. You left the pit behind — or it followed you.

signature_skills.note: Hide and leather trading

trace_skills.note: Plant materials for tanning — oak bark, specific roots
You know what a healthy hide looks like before it comes off
Working with hides teaches you what sick animals look like
Medicinal crossover with tanning plant knowledge
Working with tanning chemicals builds resistance

starting_inventory: Tanner's knife (good quality — your own)
A length of good leather cord (always useful)
A small debt to your master (recorded somewhere)
Iron chits — minimal

warm: Other tanners and leatherworkers (guild recognition)
Merchants who deal in hides (they know the trade)
cold: Everyone else, slightly — tanners carry the smell and the social stigma of their work. Not hostile, just... kept at arm's length in polite company.
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Free Fens, where peat and specific plants grow that matter to the trade, your plant knowledge is more useful than usual. In the Amber Coast, the leather trade is significant and your skills are commercially valuable.

backgrounds.background_thread: The debt to your master. It's recorded. It's not huge, but it's real. Your master is somewhere and has a legitimate claim on your labor or coin. They may pursue it or may not, depending on their character — which you know better than anyone.
backgrounds.the_weight: You will never fully escape the smell — not socially. People who know the trade know where you came from the moment they're close enough. It closes certain doors and opens others.

backgrounds.name: Carpenter's Apprentice
backgrounds.hook: You know wood — how it moves with moisture, where it's strong, where it will fail. You've framed buildings, fitted joints, made furniture and wheel spokes and coffins. Your hands show it.

trace_skills.note: You selected and felled your own timber
Foundation and mixed construction work
Timber scouting in forests
You know how to maintain tools — transfer to weapon hafts

starting_inventory: Personal carpentry tools (hammer, chisels, saw, adze — worn but quality)
A finished item you made yourself (small box, a knife handle, something personal)
Working clothes
A few bronze marks

warm: Craftsmen's guilds
Settlement administrators (carpenters are always needed for construction and repair)
cold: No specific negative standing
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Thornsveld Marches, the Marchwarden's hall has posted a carpenter need. In the Free Fens, where everything is built on stilts and boardwalks, a carpenter with fen-appropriate knowledge is genuinely rare and valued.

backgrounds.background_thread: Something you built that you're either proud of or that went wrong. A building, a bridge component, a wagon. If it stands, someone uses it and might know your work. If it failed, someone knows that too.
backgrounds.the_weight: You can look at almost any structure and see how it was built and where it will fail. This is useful and occasionally terrifying. You will find yourself unable to sleep in buildings you don't trust.

backgrounds.name: Temple Servant
backgrounds.hook: You were raised by or taken into a temple — trained in its forms, its records, its rhythms. You left, or were pushed out, or were sent away. The faith is in you whether you still practice or not.

signature_skills.note: Temples teach reading as a matter of practice
The faith you were raised in, deeply. A secondary faith at level 1.
Temple kitchens feed communities
Records, schedules, correspondence

trace_skills.note: Temple medicine is intertwined with faith practice

starting_inventory: Temple-quality robes (clean, well-made — marks you as what you were)
A small religious item (specific to your faith)
Writing materials (quill, ink, small journal)
Almost no coin — temples provide for you until they don't

warm: Temples of your faith (immediate recognition, access to community support systems)
Literate communities (clerks, merchants, scholars)
cold: Rival faith institutions (depending on how theologically aggressive your home faith is)
The criminal world (you read as a temple person — trust is slow to build)
social_standing.regional_notes: In Keth, your literacy and administrative skills make you immediately hireable by the Archive or a trading house. In the Ironmark Borderlands, a temple servant from the 'wrong' faith is in active hostile territory.

backgrounds.background_thread: Why you left the temple, or why it left you. The reason is the hook and it lives in the world — another temple person knows, an institution has a record, someone was affected. If you were expelled, someone did the expelling and may have complicated feelings about you.
backgrounds.the_weight: You are more educated than your circumstances suggest. People who deal with you for five minutes notice. Some find it reassuring. Some find it suspicious. The faith you were raised in is inside you regardless of whether you practice — its rhythms, its language, its way of seeing moral questions.

backgrounds.name: Merchant's Clerk
backgrounds.hook: You kept accounts, wrote letters, tracked inventory, and watched your employer navigate the machinery of commerce. You know how money moves. You left the desk behind — willingly or not.

signature_skills.note: Professional level — correspondence, contracts, ledgers
You've watched and participated in negotiation daily

trace_skills.note: You've seen the gap between what ledgers say and what happened
Commercial law and contracts
Trade language — fragments of a second tongue

starting_inventory: Good quality clothing (your professional wardrobe — it will wear out)
Writing kit (better than average)
A ledger of your own (small, personal — notes, contacts, things observed)
Bronze marks — several weeks of careful savings

warm: Trading houses and merchants (professional recognition)
Guild offices and administrative bodies (literate, useful)
The Guild of the Sealed Ledger (they know the type)
cold: The laboring poor (you read as educated class — trust gap)
Criminal networks (you're too legible, too traceable)
social_standing.regional_notes: On the Amber Coast, you could walk into a trading house and be hired within a day. In the Ironmark Borderlands, your skills are rare and valuable — someone wants someone who can read the logistics of a campaign.

backgrounds.background_thread: What you know. You kept accounts. You saw what went through those accounts. Some of it was legitimate. Some of it wasn't. What you know, what you can prove, and who would prefer you not be able to prove it — this is your thread. Your old employer is somewhere and may be watching.
backgrounds.the_weight: You are a person who understands systems. You will see the machinery behind things that other people experience as just the world. This is an advantage. It also makes it very hard to pretend you don't notice what's happening around you.

backgrounds.name: Rural Midwife / Herbalist
backgrounds.hook: You were the person the village sent for when someone was sick, hurt, or bringing a child into the world. Your knowledge was respected and your services never stopped being needed. Something moved you on.

signature_skills.note: Your core competency — extensive plant knowledge, regional variants

trace_skills.note: You gathered your own materials
Calming frightened people is a practiced skill
Constant exposure to illness builds resistance

starting_inventory: Herbalist's kit (packed and organized — your tools are your livelihood)
Reference notes (handwritten, organized by ailment — only useful if literate, but you know the contents either way)
Plain but clean clothing
A small stock of prepared medicines
Bronze marks — modest but real

warm: Common people everywhere (midwives are universally valued)
Furrow faith communities (they operate in the same space)
The Free Fens Moor Communion (healers are honored)
cold: Wealthy and noble households (they have their own physicians and surgeons — you're for common people)
Scorched Covenant (herbalism blurs into old ways and they don't like that)
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Free Fens, the contamination crisis makes a skilled herbalist and disease recognizer immediately relevant. In the Amber Coast, your skills are commercially viable — a good apothecary earns well.

backgrounds.background_thread: Someone you couldn't save. Or someone you saved who shouldn't have survived, and now powerful people are asking questions. The village you left has a story about why you're gone. Whether that story is true is another matter.
backgrounds.the_weight: People will always want something from you. Your skills mean you are never truly anonymous in any settlement that needs a healer — which is all of them. The pressure to stop and help when you are trying to leave is constant.

backgrounds.name: Sailor
backgrounds.hook: You've worked a deck for years — lines and canvas, harbor and open water, the particular exhaustion of a long crossing and the particular relief of landfall. Something brought you ashore and you haven't found a berth back out.

signature_skills.note: Not all sailors can swim, but you learned

trace_skills.note: Rigging work
At-sea weather reading is its own skill set
Port trading
Shipboard fights are a thing
Port pidgin — fragments of multiple languages

starting_inventory: Sailor's knife (heavy, practical)
Good rope (length of quality line — a sailor never travels without it)
Oilskin coat
A few bronze marks — end of last voyage pay

warm: Port communities and harbor workers (immediate recognition)
The Tidewarden Church (sailors are their primary community)
The Corsair Throne (they know a competent sailor when they see one)
cold: Inland communities (a sailor in farm country is a strange thing — trusted slowly)
Noble households (sailors are common labor)
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Tidewarden Anchorage, you are in your element and have more options than any other starting background. In the Ironmark Borderlands, you are very far from the sea and your core skills are largely inapplicable.

backgrounds.background_thread: The last ship, the last captain, the last voyage. Why you're on land. Maritime life creates debts, enemies, and friendships in equal measure. There is a ship somewhere whose crew knows your name, for good or ill.
backgrounds.the_weight: You are genuinely disoriented inland. Navigation by stars is a comfort at sea and almost useless in dense forest. Your skills are specialized and spectacular in their element, and limited outside it. The sea is always in the back of your mind.

backgrounds.name: Petty Criminal
backgrounds.hook: You made your living in the gaps between legitimate economies — picking pockets, running small cons, moving things for people who didn't ask about their origins. You were good enough not to get caught often. So far.

signature_skills.note: Reading urban environments is survival for you

starting_inventory: Nondescript common clothing (forgettable on purpose)
A set of small tools (lockpicks, a thin blade, things that aren't obviously what they are)
Iron chits — enough for a few days, honestly
Possibly a small outstanding warrant (player's choice — this affects starting zone options)

warm: The Hollow Road network (if introduced — they recognize the type)
The poor quarters of any city (you're from here, you know the faces)
cold: City authorities and militia (professional suspicion)
Temples (you've probably lifted something from one)
Merchants (immediate wariness)
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Tidewarden Anchorage, the shadow economy is a genuine career path and the Corsair Throne is hiring. In the Thornsveld Marches, petty crime in a small community is more exposed and more personally dangerous.

backgrounds.background_thread: The thing you did that's too big to walk away from cleanly. Petty crime is sustainable until it isn't — one job that went wrong, one mark who turned out to matter, one theft that was actually from the wrong person. The thread is whatever that is.
backgrounds.the_weight: You see exits first. You notice who's watching. You are constitutionally incapable of entering a room without cataloguing it. This is a survival habit, not a choice, and it makes you profoundly uncomfortable in places where you can't do it — deep wilderness, a lord's court, a temple you can't read.

backgrounds.name: Deserter
backgrounds.hook: You were in a professional army — not a levy, a real one, paid and trained — and you left before your term ended. The reasons were yours. The record is someone else's.

signature_skills.note: You've been avoiding military patrols since you left

trace_skills.note: You've been lying about who you are for some time

starting_inventory: Military-issue sword or spear (you kept it — it marks you if recognized)
Traveling clothes over remnants of military kit
A false name (you've been using it long enough that you almost answer to it)
Minimal coin

warm: Other deserters and outlaws (silent recognition)
The Hollow Road (they move people like you)
cold: Any military force associated with your former army — actively dangerous
Official institutions generally (you don't want to be looked at closely)
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Ironmark Borderlands, a former professional soldier is valuable and nobody asks too hard about the past. In Thornsveld Marches, which army you deserted from matters — Durn's army is local, Ironmark forces are closer than comfortable.

backgrounds.background_thread: Your former army. It exists, it is organized, and there is a record of your desertion. How actively they pursue deserters depends on the army and the current military situation. Someone in that army knows your face — an officer, a bunkmate, a man you wronged or who wronged you.
backgrounds.the_weight: Paranoia is a professional skill now. You notice uniforms. You notice insignia. You know how military patrols move and where their checkpoints are. This is useful and exhausting and it never turns off.

backgrounds.name: Hedge Farmer / Smallholder
backgrounds.hook: You owned a small piece of land — marginal, never easy, but yours. Something ended that. Debt, war, a bad run of seasons, a lord who decided your land was more useful to him than to you.

signature_skills.note: This was your life for years
Farmstead self-sufficiency — rough but real

starting_inventory: A farmer's tool selection (more complete than a farmhand's — this was your equipment)
Seeds (a small packet — you don't travel without them)
Working clothes
A document (the deed to land you no longer own, or a debt record, or something that explains what was taken)

warm: Agricultural communities (you're one of them — but with slightly more status than a pure laborer)
Furrow faith temples
cold: The lord or institution that took your land — actively hostile if encountered
Urban commercial class (you have no currency in their world)
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Free Fens, someone who knows farming and wants land to work is welcomed — there's marginal land being cleared all the time. In the Thornsveld Marches, the Durnwald Mine situation means nearby farmland has some vacancies.

backgrounds.background_thread: The land. Where it is, who has it now, and what you're going to do about that. This is not a vague thread — it's a specific piece of geography with a specific name attached to it and a specific wrong that was done. Whether you pursue it is up to you, but it will come up.
backgrounds.the_weight: You understand land the way a sailor understands water. Looking at any piece of ground, you know what it's for, what it could produce, what it would take to work it. The desire to stop moving and put something in the ground is a physical pull.

backgrounds.name: Innkeeper's Child
backgrounds.hook: You grew up behind a bar and in a kitchen — watching every kind of person pass through, hearing what people say when they think nobody's listening. The inn is gone, or you left it, or it was never going to be yours.

signature_skills.note: You've handled difficult people your entire life
You know what the rumors mean and where they come from

trace_skills.note: You heard things. You know which things are valuable.
Kept the accounts — basic only

starting_inventory: Decent clothing (presentable, not fine)
A good knife (kitchen tool)
A small notebook of contacts and information (names, notes, observations — in a personal shorthand)
Bronze marks — modest working capital

warm: Innkeepers and tavernkeepers everywhere (professional brotherhood)
Travelers and merchants (you understand them and they know it)
Common people (you're one of them, clearly)
cold: Noble households (you're trade class)
Military institutions (you're civilian and read as such)
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Tidewarden Anchorage, you are in the information-rich environment you were born for. In the Ironmark Borderlands, an innkeeper's child who can run a common room is a genuine practical asset.

backgrounds.background_thread: The inn. Why it's not yours anymore, or why you left it. Someone still runs it, or it's abandoned, or it burned. Whatever happened there before you left is the thread — a specific event, a specific person, something you heard that you can't unhear.
backgrounds.the_weight: You read people faster than you read situations. This is usually an asset. Occasionally it means you see something in someone's face that you would have been better off not seeing, and now you have to decide what to do about it.

backgrounds.name: Shepherd
backgrounds.hook: You spent years alone in high pasture and remote grazing land — you, a flock, and whatever weather the sky decided to send. You know solitude the way most people know crowds.

signature_skills.note: Extended solo time in open country

trace_skills.note: Following strays, following predators
Standard shepherd weapon against wolves — treat as thrown weapons sub-skill

starting_inventory: Shepherd's crook (staff — functional weapon if needed)
Sling and stones
Weatherproof cloak (heavy, well-made — you needed it)
Tinderbox
Iron chits — minimal (shepherds don't earn much)

warm: Agricultural communities
Karn Clans (pastoral culture — immediate common ground)
Old Ways practitioners
cold: Urban communities (you are very obviously rural)
Institutions of all kinds (you have no relationship with them)
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Thornsveld Marches Karn Clan territory, you are read as a kindred type immediately. In the Free Fens, your solo wilderness competence is respected and your animal knowledge is useful. In any city, you are deeply uncomfortable and it shows.

backgrounds.background_thread: The flock. Who it belonged to, where it was, what happened to it. A shepherd without a flock is a specific kind of loss — the animals were your company and your responsibility for years. Whether you left willingly or the flock was taken, destroyed, or sold matters.
backgrounds.the_weight: Silence is not uncomfortable to you. Other people's need to fill silence is. You will sometimes simply stop talking in the middle of a conversation because you've said what needed saying. This reads as strange to urbanized characters and as perfectly normal to other rural types.

backgrounds.name: Impoverished Minor Noble
backgrounds.hook: Your family had a name once. Land, maybe. A seat at someone's table. It's gone — spent, lost, seized, or inherited in such reduced form that 'noble' is now a word people use to politely describe your good breeding and visible poverty.

signature_skills.note: This is the one inheritance that can't be taken
Lineage, heraldry, family history — your education covered this obsessively
You learned before the horse was sold

trace_skills.note: Property law and inheritance specifically
Gentleman's training — real but not professional

starting_inventory: Good but worn clothing (clearly once-fine — this is a statement and a liability)
A family seal or signet (the name still means something somewhere, even if you're not sure where)
A sword (the family sword — not the best weapon in the world but it's old and it's yours)
Almost no coin — this is the defining feature of your situation

warm: Noble households (the name gets you in the door — what you do there is up to you)
Literate and educated class
cold: Common laborers (class resentment is real)
Merchants (they see the poverty through the manners and they know you need them more than they need you)
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Thornsveld Marches, a noble name might mean something specific in the succession politics. In the Amber Coast, the Factorate is full of people like you — useful, educated, poor.

backgrounds.background_thread: The family name. What it means, where it's recognized, and what happened to the estate or title. There are legal claims, possibly, that have never been pursued. There are people who remember your family well and others who remember them badly. The name is an asset and a target.
backgrounds.the_weight: You are trained for a life you cannot currently access and untrained for the life you're actually living. The gap between how you were taught to move in the world and what the world currently offers you is a constant friction. Learning to do labor without telegraphing that you've never done it before is its own skill.

backgrounds.name: Hedge Scholar / Failed Student
backgrounds.hook: You were educated beyond your station — by a temple, a patron, a scholarly institution, or a parent who believed in it. You never arrived at the life that education was meant to prepare you for. You know things and have very little to show for it.

signature_skills.note: Genuine reading and writing, not just basic literacy
Academic knowledge of multiple faiths
At least one additional language to functional level

trace_skills.note: One region studied deeply

starting_inventory: A modest library (three to five books or manuscripts — your most valued possession and your most impractical one to carry)
Writing materials (good quality)
Plain clothing
A letter of introduction to someone who may or may not remember writing it

warm: The First Scholars / Archive of Keth (if you reach it)
Literate and administrative classes
Temples of the Reckoning and Furrow faiths (they value literacy)
cold: Working trades (you are immediately legible as someone who has never done physical work)
Military institutions (what use are you, exactly?)
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Keth Plateau, the Archive of Keth is the center of the known world for someone like you — and also a place where your knowledge will be immediately evaluated and found either valuable or wanting. In the Amber Coast, literate people find work.

backgrounds.background_thread: The education and what interrupted it. A patron who withdrew support, an institution that expelled you, a war that ended the school, a personal failing, or simply the reality that the position you were educated for doesn't exist anymore. The people who educated you are somewhere and have opinions about what you did with it.
backgrounds.the_weight: You know the shape of things you can't afford to touch. You understand the Archive and what it contains. You understand the law and can't afford a lawyer. You understand economics and have no capital. Knowledge without power is its own specific frustration.

backgrounds.name: Fen-Born
backgrounds.hook: You grew up in the Free Fens — in a village on a raised platform, learning to read the bog before you learned to read anything else. The Fens are yours in the way that no other place ever will be.

signature_skills.note: You can move through fen terrain that would kill most travelers
Fen-specific plant knowledge is deep
Moor Communion herbalism is extensive

trace_skills.note: Starting fire in wet conditions is its own skill
Moor Communion — you grew up inside it
Moving quietly in the fen

starting_inventory: Fen-appropriate clothing (practical, waterproofed, muted colors)
Long knife
Pole (navigating shallow fen water and testing ground)
Tinderbox (special fen-use design — works damp)
Dried fen provisions (specific foods, small pack)

warm: Free Fens communities (you are one of them)
Moor Communion Stations (immediate welcome)
Barefoot Compact wanderers (you share values)
cold: Urban environments (you are visibly, unmistakably from somewhere else)
Organized military (the Fens don't produce soldiers by temperament)
Ashenveld agents (your people have reasons to dislike them)
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Free Fens, you are in your element and have advantages no other background provides. Outside the Fens, almost everything that makes you exceptional at home is useless.

backgrounds.background_thread: The contamination in the northwest fen. You know those waters. You know that smell and you know it shouldn't be there. Whether you left before or after it started, you know more about what it might mean than anyone outside the Fens does.
backgrounds.the_weight: The further you get from water and open sky, the more closed-in you feel. Cities are difficult. Dense forests feel wrong in a different way from the fen. You are genuinely one of the most competent people in the world in one specific environment and competent-below-average in most others.

backgrounds.name: Hollow Road Runner
backgrounds.hook: You worked for the Hollow Road — moving goods, carrying messages, occasionally making sure someone had a safe place to sleep who couldn't use the official ones. You weren't management. You were a route.

signature_skills.note: You know roads that aren't on any map
Lying to checkpoint guards is a professional skill

trace_skills.note: Courier work

starting_inventory: Traveler's clothing (deliberately forgettable)
A hollow-handled knife (small concealment space inside handle — may or may not have anything in it)
A route map (hand-drawn, coded in a simple cipher only Hollow Road people recognize — worthless or invaluable depending on who you show it to)
Iron chits and possibly one bronze mark

warm: The Hollow Road network (anywhere there's a node)
Outlaws and people who need to move quietly
cold: Official institutions everywhere (you've been actively working against their ability to track movement)
The Scorched Covenant (they've tried to shut down Hollow Road operations in Ironmark territories)
social_standing.regional_notes: In the Ironmark Borderlands, the Hollow Road is a real operational presence and you have contacts. In the Free Fens, Hollow Road uses the fen routes and you may know the Fens-side contacts. In the Tidewarden Anchorage, the shadow economy and Hollow Road overlap considerably.

backgrounds.background_thread: A run that went wrong, or something you moved that you now wish you hadn't, or a contact who disappeared. The Hollow Road is not a safe employer — it is a useful one. What you know about its operations, its routes, and its people is both your asset and your vulnerability.
backgrounds.the_weight: You are always thinking about exits. Not in the petty criminal way of cataloguing rooms — in the operational way of knowing every road, every back path, every place a person could go to ground. You don't think of this as unusual. Other people find it unsettling.
